title: Research
text:
BAHE investigates how interactions among organisms, environments and people shape biodiversity, wildlife health, ecosystem functioning and sustainable futures.
We combine ecological observation, field and molecular approaches, quantitative modelling and interdisciplinary synthesis to understand ecological change across biological and socioecological systems.

title: Research
text:
Research programmes


Biodiversity under global change
We study how species, communities and ecosystems respond to land-use change, fragmentation, urbanisation, restoration and conservation intervention.

title: Research
text:
Biodiversity under global change
We study how biodiversity responds to environmental change across landscapes and through time. Our work examines the consequences of land-use change, habitat fragmentation, urbanisation, protected-area management, renewable-energy infrastructure, restoration and conservation intervention.

Example questions

How do species and ecological communities respond to changing landscapes?
Which processes determine persistence, turnover, recovery and resilience?
How can monitoring distinguish short-term variation from directional change?
Which conservation and restoration actions produce durable ecological benefits?


land-use change community turnover restoration biodiversity monitoring

title: Research
section: Wildlife ecology, health and One Health
text:
Wildlife ecology, health and One Health
We investigate wildlife populations, host–parasite interactions, microbiomes and disease processes within their ecological and environmental contexts. This research links biodiversity science with One Health by examining how changes in habitats, communities and ecological interactions affect wildlife health, pathogen dynamics and potential risks to people.

Example questions

How does host-community structure influence parasite and pathogen dynamics?
How do land-use change and habitat disturbance affect wildlife health?
How do parasites, microbiomes and host traits interact?
How can ecological evidence strengthen One Health surveillance and conservation?


wildlife health host–parasite interactions microbiomes One Health

title: Research
text:
Ecological interactions and system dynamics
Ecological systems are shaped by interactions that unfold across space and time. We study how colonisation, priority effects, succession, ecological networks, species associations and environmental feedbacks influence community assembly and ecosystem dynamics.
Our work combines empirical research, simulation and statistical modelling to examine how interactions can be detected, interpreted and linked to ecological mechanisms.

Research interests

Community assembly and priority effects
Species co-occurrence and interaction inference
Host–parasite and multispecies networks
Temporal ecological dynamics
Ecological resilience and alternative system states
Biodiversity offsetting and restoration trajectories


community assembly priority effects ecological networks system dynamics

title: Research
text:
Nature, wellbeing and sustainable futures
We examine how relationships between people and natural environments can be understood within broader ecological, One Health and sustainability frameworks. This includes research on nature-based wellbeing, ecological integrity, environmental pressures, equitable access to nature and the long-term sustainability of nature-based interventions.

Example questions

How does ecological quality influence the benefits people derive from nature?
Which biodiversity-mediated pathways connect ecosystems and human wellbeing?
How can nature-based interventions support health without degrading ecological integrity?
How should restoration, environmental pressures and equity be incorporated into sustainability assessment?


nature-based wellbeing ecological integrity socioecological systems sustainable futures

title: Research
text:
Research across landscapes
BAHE research spans tropical, urban, agricultural and otherwise human-modified systems. Long-standing interests include biodiversity and wildlife health in Borneo and other biodiversity-rich regions, alongside comparative research across Europe, Southeast Asia, Australasia and global evidence networks.
These settings provide opportunities to investigate ecological processes under contrasting levels of disturbance, management and environmental change.


Tropical systems
Biodiversity, wildlife health, habitat disturbance and conservation across biodiversity-rich tropical landscapes.


Urban and peri-urban systems
Species, communities, microbiomes and health dynamics in increasingly human-dominated environments.


Conservation and production landscapes
Protected areas, agricultural systems, restoration sites and landscapes shaped by infrastructure and resource use.


Global comparative research
Evidence synthesis, meta-analysis and harmonised ecological datasets connecting patterns across regions and systems.

title: Research
text:
Methods and analytical approaches
We develop and apply methods that connect research questions, sampling design, data structure, analysis and ecological interpretation. Our emphasis is not only on predictive performance, but also on uncertainty, mechanism and transparent inference.


Field ecology and monitoring
Study design, wildlife sampling, biodiversity surveys, habitat assessment and long-term ecological monitoring.


Molecular and biodiversity data
Molecular ecology, host-associated data, microbiome and parasite information, biodiversity databases and integrated ecological datasets.


Quantitative ecology
R programming, Bayesian and hierarchical modelling, occupancy models, simulation, ecological networks and reproducible analysis.


Evidence synthesis
Systematic and targeted review methods, meta-analysis, structured data extraction and conceptual synthesis.


Spatial and temporal analysis
Species distribution modelling, remote sensing, longitudinal data, landscape analysis and ecological forecasting.


Interdisciplinary frameworks
One Health, socioecological systems, conservation decision support and integrated sustainability assessment.

title: Research
text:
Conservation evidence and ecosystem resilience
BAHE contributes to applied conservation by generating evidence on biodiversity change, ecological resilience, restoration and the consequences of disturbance and management.
We are particularly interested in work that connects ecological understanding with practical decisions while recognising uncertainty, trade-offs and the temporal dynamics of ecological recovery.
Potential applications include:

Biodiversity monitoring and evaluation
Conservation and restoration planning
Ecological impact assessment
Wildlife-health surveillance
Biodiversity offsetting
Nature-based solutions
Integrated One Health and sustainability assessment

title: Research
text:
Methods and training environment
BAHE provides a research-led environment for developing expertise in ecological fieldwork, molecular and biodiversity data, statistical modelling, evidence synthesis and interdisciplinary systems thinking.
Training is embedded within active research projects, collaborative analysis, supervision, manuscript development and the creation of transparent and reproducible scientific workflows.

Research philosophy: Curiosity about the natural world is the starting point of scientific discovery and the enduring motivation for understanding how life responds to environmental change. BAHE combines field observation, conceptual thinking, quantitative analysis and interdisciplinary synthesis to reveal the interactions that connect organisms, ecosystems and people across scales. We seek to develop independent scientists who pursue research with intellectual curiosity, analytical rigour and scientific integrity, generating knowledge that advances ecology while providing evidence to support biodiversity conservation, ecosystem resilience and more informed decisions for sustainable futures..

title: Research
text:
Collaboration, training and knowledge exchange
BAHE welcomes collaboration with researchers, conservation organisations, public bodies, health specialists, businesses and policy partners.

title: Optimizing Biodiversity Offsetting for Habitat Succession and Colonization Dynamics
text:
Research article

Optimizing Biodiversity Offsetting for Habitat Succession and Colonization Dynamics


Konstans Wells, Luca Börger, Miguel Lurgi, Kevin Watts, James M. Bullock


Full citation

Wells, K., Börger, L., Lurgi, M., Watts, K., & Bullock, J. M. (2026). Optimizing biodiversity offsetting for habitat succession and colonization dynamics. Conservation Biology, e70362.



Publisher article



About this knowledge page. This BAHE Research Record provides curated notes and links for the peer-reviewed publication. It supports discovery and interpretation but does not replace the publisher-hosted version of record, which remains the authoritative scientific source and should be cited as the primary reference.








Research overview


The publication advances biodiversity offset science by integrating habitat succession, species colonization dynamics and repeated development–offset cycles within a single ecological modelling framework. It demonstrates that offset effectiveness emerges from interactions among spatial compensation, conservation duration, landscape dynamics and species-specific ecological responses rather than from fixed compensation ratios alone. The study identifies ecological recovery time, habitat conversion rate and long-term protection as fundamental determinants of successful biodiversity offsetting and provides a transferable framework for evaluating conservation policy under dynamic landscape change.





Knowledge statements


Interpretive statements that distil key ideas and propositions from the publication to support scholarly reflection and discussion. They should be read alongside the complete peer-reviewed article rather than as standalone conclusions.


Knowledge statements are interpretive discussion points derived from the publication. They are intended to support exploration, comparison, and scholarly dialogue across the BAHE knowledge base. Individual statements are intentionally concise and should not be interpreted independently of the complete peer-reviewed publication, which remains the authoritative source for the authors arguments, evidence, and conclusions.






Ecological recovery time fundamentally determines whether biodiversity offsets achieve long-term conservation outcomes.





Offset performance emerges from interactions among habitat succession, species colonization and landscape dynamics rather than from compensation ratios alone..





Short-term evaluations can substantially overestimate biodiversity offset success because ecological time lags delay recovery.





Biodiversity offset policy should explicitly account for ecological timescales, multiple biodiversity indicators and long-term conservation commitments.





Spatio-temporally explicit simulation provides an effective framework for evaluating biodiversity offset strategies under dynamic landscapes.





Biodiversity offset performance depends on multiple components of biodiversity responding differently to restoration, highlighting the need to evaluate offset success using complementary ecological indicators rather than a single measure.





Biodiversity offset schemes that appear successful at individual restoration sites may nevertheless fail to achieve long-term biodiversity conservation when evaluated across dynamic landscapes and repeated impact–offset cycles.






Original abstract


Abstract from the peer-reviewed publication.


Biodiversity offsetting aims to compensate for ecological losses from development through conservation and restoration. Using a conceptual forest succession model, the study evaluated how offset outcomes depend on compensation scale, duration of protection, habitat conversion rate and land availability constraints. Effective offsetting required restoring areas larger than those impacted together with long-term protection aligned with ecological recovery. High habitat conversion rates consistently reduced offset success, especially for colonizing species dependent on habitat quality and long-term protection. The study demonstrates that biodiversity offset policies must explicitly incorporate ecological time lags, landscape dynamics and multiple biodiversity indicators to avoid overestimating long-term conservation outcomes. :contentReferenceoaicite:0

title: Mixed Effects of Protected Areas on Avian Food Webs
text:
Research article

Mixed Effects of Protected Areas on Avian Food Webs


Lucie Thompson, Nuria Galiana, Konstans Wells, Miguel Lurgi


Full citation

Thompson, L., Galiana, N., Wells, K., & Lurgi, M. (2025). Mixed effects of protected areas on avian food webs. Proceedings of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences, 292, 20250614. https://doi.org/10.1098/rspb.2025.0614



Publisher article



About this knowledge page. This BAHE Research Record provides curated notes and links for the peer-reviewed publication. It supports discovery and interpretation but does not replace the publisher-hosted version of record, which remains the authoritative scientific source and should be cited as the primary reference.




Protected-area networks across Europe used to evaluate differences in avian food-web structure inside and outside protected areas.




Research overview


The publication advances protected-area assessment beyond species counts by analysing ecological interaction networks at continental scale. It shows that protected areas do not produce a single consistent food-web response: protection can support larger predators, longer food chains and more complex trophic structure in some contexts, while effects on many other metrics are neutral or heterogeneous. The results identify landscape context and designation purpose as important mediators of protection outcomes and demonstrate that ecological-network metrics reveal conservation effects that species richness alone cannot capture.





Knowledge statements


Interpretive statements that distil key ideas and propositions from the publication to support scholarly reflection and discussion. They should be read alongside the complete peer-reviewed article rather than as standalone conclusions.


Knowledge statements are interpretive discussion points derived from the publication. They are intended to support exploration, comparison, and scholarly dialogue across the BAHE knowledge base. Individual statements are intentionally concise and should not be interpreted independently of the complete peer-reviewed publication, which remains the authoritative source for the authors arguments, evidence, and conclusions.






European protected areas have mixed effects on avian food-web structure, with positive, neutral and negative outcomes varying among protected-area networks and food-web properties.





Protected areas are associated overall with higher avian species richness and larger-bodied intermediate and top trophic species than nearby non-protected areas.





Most avian food-web metrics show no uniform continental-scale response to protection, despite substantial variation in protection effects among individual protected-area networks.





Protected areas can support longer and more connected avian food chains by harbouring larger top predators that link multiple trophic pathways.





The geographical and environmental context of protected areas, including elevation, slope, habitat diversity, remoteness, agriculture and human pressure, influences their effects on avian food-web structure.





Protected-area networks containing a higher proportion of sites managed specifically for birds tend to sustain longer food chains and food webs with fewer but more highly positioned top species.





Habitat heterogeneity within protected-area networks is associated with larger-bodied top predators, indicating that diverse land-cover mosaics can support higher trophic levels.





Greater urban development and human pressure within protected areas can reduce food-web connectivity and shorten food chains even when local species richness is comparatively high.





Assessments based only on species richness can overlook changes in trophic structure, species interactions and other dimensions of ecological community integrity.





Protected-area effectiveness for ecological networks depends on both conditions inside reserves and the ecological integrity of surrounding landscapes.





Conservation planning should incorporate explicit management objectives, environmental context and ecological interaction metrics when designing and evaluating protected areas.





Limited continental-scale interaction data, taxonomic biases in citizen science records and assumptions that co-occurring species realize known trophic links constrain current assessments of protected-area effects on ecological networks.






Original abstract


Abstract from the peer-reviewed publication.


Protecting habitat and the species they shelter by setting up protected areas (PAs) has become a conservation priority to mitigate the current extinction crisis. This strategy has improved different aspects of biodiversity including species richness and abundance across ecosystems. However, to truly understand the effectiveness of mitigation measures against global environmental change, we must account for one of the fundamental dimensions of biodiversity: species interactions. Using 376 556 curated citizen science records of 509 bird species distributed across 45 networks of European PAs (collections of connected PAs), we show that the effects of PAs on the structure of avian food webs are mixed across Europe. Overall effects of protection include an increase in species richness and larger body masses of both top and intermediate species. For other food web features, the sign and magnitude of the effects are mixed. Our results further suggest that these effects are strongly influenced by geographical and environmental features of the PA networks such as remoteness, habitat diversity, human pressure and agriculture. Lastly, PAs with specific protection goals such as those administered by European Bird Directives for conservation sustain more complex food webs. Our study provides evidence for the need of including clear management goals and considering environmental context in the designation of PAs to increase their effectiveness at preserving biodiversity beyond species richness.

title: Triadic Relationships Between Pasture Exposure, Gastrointestinal Parasites, and Hindgut Microbiomes in Grazing Lambs
text:
Research article

Triadic Relationships Between Pasture Exposure, Gastrointestinal Parasites, and Hindgut Microbiomes in Grazing Lambs


Jack Jefferson, Claire Reigate, Alessandra Giacomini, M. Jordana Rivero, Matthew Hitchings, Tamsyn Uren Webster, Konstans Wells


Full citation

Jefferson, J., Reigate, C., Giacomini, A., Rivero, M. J., Hitchings, M., Uren Webster, T., & Wells, K. (2025). Triadic relationships between pasture exposure, gastrointestinal parasites, and hindgut microbiomes in grazing lambs. PLOS One, 20(11), e0337086. https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0337086



Publisher article



About this knowledge page. This BAHE Research Record provides curated notes and links for the peer-reviewed publication. It supports discovery and interpretation but does not replace the publisher-hosted version of record, which remains the authoritative scientific source and should be cited as the primary reference.




Ordination of gut microbiome composition in lambs grazing on two sward types across three consecutive months.




Research overview


The publication integrates pasture ecology, parasitology and microbiome science within a naturally infected grazing-livestock system. It shows that host-specific microbiome signatures remain dominant while environmental exposure and gastrointestinal nematode burden influence the abundance of particular bacterial taxa. The overlap between taxa associated with pasture, season and parasite burden provides evidence that microbiome dynamics emerge from interacting host, environmental and pathological processes rather than from any single driver.





Knowledge statements


Interpretive statements that distil key ideas and propositions from the publication to support scholarly reflection and discussion. They should be read alongside the complete peer-reviewed article rather than as standalone conclusions.


Knowledge statements are interpretive discussion points derived from the publication. They are intended to support exploration, comparison, and scholarly dialogue across the BAHE knowledge base. Individual statements are intentionally concise and should not be interpreted independently of the complete peer-reviewed publication, which remains the authoritative source for the authors arguments, evidence, and conclusions.






Hindgut microbiome composition is strongly individual-specific, suggesting that established microbial communities remain relatively stable while exhibiting adaptive responses to variation in pasture conditions and gastrointestinal parasite burden.





Pasture composition, seasonal conditions and gastrointestinal nematode burden each influence the relative abundance of specific gut bacterial taxa, but have comparatively modest effects on overall microbiome structure.





Lambs grazing mixed-species swards carry higher gastrointestinal nematode burdens than lambs grazing ryegrass-dominated pastures, demonstrating that pasture composition influences parasite exposure.





Many bacterial taxa associated with gastrointestinal nematode burden also vary with pasture type or season, providing evidence for interconnected relationships between environmental exposure, parasites and the hindgut microbiome.





Grazing lambs maintain a relatively stable core hindgut microbiome while exhibiting adaptive shifts in the abundance of functionally important bacterial taxa in response to environmental and parasitological conditions.





Understanding livestock gut microbiomes requires an integrated ecological framework that jointly considers host individuality, pasture conditions, parasite burden and developmental timing rather than evaluating these drivers independently.






Original abstract


Abstract from the peer-reviewed publication.


Livestock grazing in confined pastures often means grazing on a less diverse diet than under more natural conditions and increased exposure to gastrointestinal parasites prevailing in these pastures. However, how sward composition influences gut microbiome diversity and its relationship with parasite burden remains poorly understood. In this study, we analysed the faecal gut microbiome of weaned lambs grazing on two distinct sward types (perennial ryegrass and a mixed-species sward) over three consecutive months using 16S rRNA sequencing, in order to assess how microbial diversity and composition are related to environmental conditions and the gastrointestinal nematode burden in naturally infected lambs. Sward type and sampling time explained some of the variation in gut microbiome alpha diversity and community composition, whereas individual lamb identity accounted for considerably more variation in microbial assemblages. Shifts in the relative abundance of bacterial genera such as Saccharofermentans, Anaerosporobacter and Butyrivibrio in relation to sward type and sampling time suggest mostly adaptive fluctuations in response to diet and pasture condition. Abundance shifts of Negativibacillus and Candidatus Saccharimonas were also associated with gastrointestinal nematode burden, which, in turn, was higher in lambs grazing on mixed swards compared to ryegrass. Our findings add to the growing understanding of how sheep microbiomes vary with pasture management and changes in parasite burden. We highlight that individual identity may shape gut microbiota, and that potential triadic interactions among gastrointestinal parasites, sward exposure, and the gut microbiome underscore the importance of considering host, parasite, and environmental factors collectively when evaluating microbiome dynamics in grazing livestock.

title: A Cyst-Forming Coccidian With Large Geographical Range Infecting Forest and Commensal Rodents: Sarcocystis muricoelognathis sp. nov.
text:
Research article

A Cyst-Forming Coccidian With Large Geographical Range Infecting Forest and Commensal Rodents: Sarcocystis muricoelognathis sp. nov.


Tao Qin, Paula Ortega-Perez, Gudrun Wibbelt, Maklarin B. Lakim, Sulaiman Ginting, Yuvaluk Khoprasert, Konstans Wells, Junjie Hu, Thomas Jäkel


Full citation

Qin, T., Ortega-Perez, P., Wibbelt, G., Lakim, M. B., Ginting, S., Khoprasert, Y., Wells, K., Hu, J., & Jäkel, T. (2024). A cyst-forming coccidian with large geographical range infecting forest and commensal rodents: Sarcocystis muricoelognathis sp. nov. Parasites & Vectors, 17, 135. https://doi.org/10.1186/s13071-024-06230-8



Publisher article



About this knowledge page. This BAHE Research Record provides curated notes and links for the peer-reviewed publication. It supports discovery and interpretation but does not replace the publisher-hosted version of record, which remains the authoritative scientific source and should be cited as the primary reference.



Research overview


The publication resolves a cryptic Sarcocystis lineage by integrating transmission experiments, host records, comparative morphology, transmission electron microscopy and multiple genetic markers. It demonstrates that a single parasite species can bridge commensal and forest-dwelling rodent communities across a large geographical range and shows that species delimitation within the S. zuoi complex requires multilocus evidence rather than reliance on highly conserved markers.





Knowledge statements


Interpretive statements that distil key ideas and propositions from the publication to support scholarly reflection and discussion. They should be read alongside the complete peer-reviewed article rather than as standalone conclusions.


Knowledge statements are interpretive discussion points derived from the publication. They are intended to support exploration, comparison, and scholarly dialogue across the BAHE knowledge base. Individual statements are intentionally concise and should not be interpreted independently of the complete peer-reviewed publication, which remains the authoritative source for the authors arguments, evidence, and conclusions.






Sarcocystis muricoelognathis sp. nov. is a distinct cyst-forming coccidian within the Sarcocystis zuoi species complex.





Sarcocystis muricoelognathis occurs across Thailand, Borneo and southwestern China, demonstrating a broad geographical distribution in eastern and southeastern Asia.





Rat snakes Coelognathus radiatus and Coelognathus flavolineatus act as definitive hosts capable of transmitting Sarcocystis muricoelognathis to rodent intermediate hosts.





Experimental inoculation of Sprague-Dawley rats with sporocysts from naturally infected rat snakes produced mature sarcocysts in striated muscle tissue.





Sarcocysts from experimental rats in Thailand, wild Rattus norvegicus in China and Maxomys whiteheadi in Borneo share a distinctive type 10a cyst-wall ultrastructure and morphologically similar cystozoites.





Sarcocystis muricoelognathis infects both commensal rodents and forest-dwelling small mammals, indicating host shifts across ecological settings.





The nuclear 18S rRNA gene alone cannot reliably distinguish Sarcocystis muricoelognathis from Sarcocystis zuoi because the two species are highly similar at this locus.





ITS1 and 28S rRNA sequence data provide stronger discriminatory power than 18S rRNA or mitochondrial cox1 for separating closely related species in the Sarcocystis zuoi complex.





A Thai isolate previously identified as Sarcocystis zuoi under accession KU341120 is most likely conspecific with Sarcocystis muricoelognathis.





Mitochondrial cox1 sequences are nearly identical among several members of the Sarcocystis zuoi complex, suggesting recent shared ancestry and limited resolution for species delimitation.





Sarcocystis sequences recovered from Sundasciurus lowii indicate that Sarcocystis scandentiborneensis may infect squirrels as well as treeshrews.





Known host associations of rodent-associated Sarcocystis species are likely incomplete snapshots of more extensive parasite–host networks.






Original abstract


Abstract from the peer-reviewed publication.


Background: The geographic distribution and host-parasite interaction networks of Sarcocystis spp. in small mammals in eastern Asia remain incompletely known. Methods: Experimental infections, morphological and molecular characterizations were used for discrimination of a new Sarcocystis species isolated from colubrid snakes and small mammals collected in Thailand, Borneo and China. Results: We identified a new species, Sarcocystis muricoelognathis sp. nov., that features a relatively wide geographic distribution and infects both commensal and forest-inhabiting intermediate hosts. Sarcocystis sporocysts collected from rat snakes (Coelognathus radiatus, C. flavolineatus) in Thailand induced development of sarcocysts in experimental SD rats showing a type 10a cyst wall ultrastructure that was identical with those found in Rattus norvegicus from China and the forest rat Maxomys whiteheadi in Borneo. Its cystozoites had equal sizes in all intermediate hosts and locations, while sporocysts and cystozoites were distinct from other Sarcocystis species. Partial 28S rRNA sequences of S. muricoelognathis from M. whiteheadi were largely identical to those from R. norvegicus in China but distinct from newly sequenced Sarcocystis zuoi. The phylogeny of the nuclear 18S rRNA gene placed S. muricoelognathis within the so-called S. zuoi complex, including Sarcocystis attenuati, S. kani, S. scandentiborneensis and S. zuoi, while the latter clustered with the new species. However, the phylogeny of the ITS1-region confirmed the distinction between S. muricoelognathis and S. zuoi. Moreover, all three gene trees suggested that an isolate previously addressed as S. zuoi from Thailand (KU341120) is conspecific with S. muricoelognathis. Partial mitochondrial cox1 sequences of S. muricoelognathis were almost identical with those from other members of the group suggesting a shared, recent ancestry. Additionally, we isolated two partial 28S rRNA Sarcocystis sequences from Low's squirrel Sundasciurus lowii that clustered with those of S. scandentiborneensis from treeshrews. Conclusions: Our results provide strong evidence of broad geographic distributions of rodent-associated Sarcocystis and host shifts between commensal and forest small mammal species, even if the known host associations remain likely only snapshots of the true associations.

title: A systematic review and guide for using multi-response statistical models in co-infection research
text:
Review Article

A systematic review and guide for using multi-response statistical models in co-infection research


Francisca Powell-Romero, Konstans Wells, Nicholas J. Clark


Full citation

Francisca Powell-Romero, Konstans Wells, Nicholas J. Clark (2024). A systematic review and guide for using multi-response statistical models in co-infection research. Royal Society Open Science 11 231589 https://doi.org/10.1098/rsos.231589



Publisher article



About this knowledge page. This BAHE Research Record provides curated notes and links for the peer-reviewed publication. It supports discovery and interpretation but does not replace the publisher-hosted version of record, which remains the authoritative scientific source and should be cited as the primary reference.



Research overview


The review synthesizes current practice in multi-response modelling for co-infection studies, identifies clear methodological divergence between ecology and epidemiology, and proposes opportunities for methodological integration to strengthen infectious disease research.





Knowledge statements


Interpretive statements that distil key ideas and propositions from the publication to support scholarly reflection and discussion. They should be read alongside the complete peer-reviewed article rather than as standalone conclusions.


Knowledge statements are interpretive discussion points derived from the publication. They are intended to support exploration, comparison, and scholarly dialogue across the BAHE knowledge base. Individual statements are intentionally concise and should not be interpreted independently of the complete peer-reviewed publication, which remains the authoritative source for the authors arguments, evidence, and conclusions.






Multi-response statistical models should be selected according to the scientific question rather than disciplinary convention.





Epidemiology studies predominantly use multinomial models for risk-factor and spatial-distribution analyses.





Ecology studies predominantly use multivariate models to investigate pathogen associations and community structure.





Citation-network analysis demonstrates limited knowledge exchange between ecology and epidemiology.





Cross-disciplinary integration could improve One Health research and disease management.





Multivariate models provide opportunities to incorporate conditional pathogen dependencies into prediction.





Spatial information remains underused in ecological multivariate co-infection studies.





Greater sharing of modelling approaches would improve inference and prediction.

title: Supporting Bird Diversity and Ecological Function in Managed Grassland and Forest Systems Needs an Integrative Approach
text:
Research article

Supporting Bird Diversity and Ecological Function in Managed Grassland and Forest Systems Needs an Integrative Approach


Kirsten Jung, Miriam Teuscher, Stefan Böhm, Konstans Wells, Manfred Ayasse, Markus Fischer, Wolfgang W. Weisser, Swen C. Renner, Marco Tschapka


Full citation

Jung, K., Teuscher, M., Böhm, S., Wells, K., Ayasse, M., Fischer, M., Weisser, W. W., Renner, S. C., & Tschapka, M. (2024). Supporting bird diversity and ecological function in managed grassland and forest systems needs an integrative approach. Frontiers in Environmental Science, 12, 1401513. https://doi.org/10.3389/fenvs.2024.1401513



Publisher article



About this knowledge page. This BAHE Research Record provides curated notes and links for the peer-reviewed publication. It supports discovery and interpretation but does not replace the publisher-hosted version of record, which remains the authoritative scientific source and should be cited as the primary reference.




Functional richness, dispersion (trait dissimilarity), and evenness (homogeneity of trait abundances) across trait dimensions with increasing species diversity in grasslands and forests. Credit: Kirsten Jung




Research overview


The publication advances trait-based biodiversity assessment by integrating multiple functional dimensions with detailed land-management data across two major production ecosystems. It demonstrates that bird responses cannot be explained by a single management-intensity gradient because ecosystem type, regional context, stand structure and surrounding habitat interact to shape taxonomic and functional diversity. The study also shows that ecological and behavioural traits are particularly informative for understanding community assembly, while acoustic and morphometric traits reveal additional management-related constraints.





Knowledge statements


Interpretive statements that distil key ideas and propositions from the publication to support scholarly reflection and discussion. They should be read alongside the complete peer-reviewed article rather than as standalone conclusions.


Knowledge statements are interpretive discussion points derived from the publication. They are intended to support exploration, comparison, and scholarly dialogue across the BAHE knowledge base. Individual statements are intentionally concise and should not be interpreted independently of the complete peer-reviewed publication, which remains the authoritative source for the authors arguments, evidence, and conclusions.






Bird species diversity and functional richness are substantially lower in managed grasslands than in managed forests across the three German Biodiversity Exploratories regions.





Bird species diversity is positively associated with functional richness and functional dispersion in both forest and grassland systems.





Increasing grassland land-use intensity reduces bird species diversity independently of whether sites are managed as pastures, meadows or mown pastures.





The strength of bird-diversity responses to grassland land-use intensity differs among regions, demonstrating that management effects are geographically context dependent.





Forest tree species, stand age and structural heterogeneity influence bird species diversity and functional composition more consistently than a single overall measure of silvicultural management intensity.





Mature and structurally heterogeneous forest stands generally support higher bird species diversity than younger pole-stage stands.





Bird species composition and trait composition are more strongly coupled in forests than in grasslands, indicating stronger trait-based environmental sorting in forest communities.





Ecological and behavioural traits explain grassland bird assemblage composition more strongly than morphometric or acoustic traits.





Acoustic, ecological, behavioural and morphometric trait dimensions each reveal distinct constraints through which management and habitat structure shape bird assemblages.





Woody vegetation in the immediate landscape surrounding managed grasslands contributes to the maintenance of bird diversity and functional composition.





Conservation assessments based only on local species richness can overlook functional changes, ecological sorting and biotic homogenization within managed production landscapes.





Effective bird conservation in production landscapes requires coordinated local and regional management that maintains complementary grassland, forest and woody habitat elements.






Original abstract


Abstract from the peer-reviewed publication.


In modified production landscapes, biodiversity faces unprecedented pressures from human actions, resulting in significant species declines of plant and animal taxa, including birds. Understanding the underlying mechanisms responsible for such declines is essential to counteract further loss and support practitioners in conserving biodiversity and associated ecosystem function. In this study, we used standardized bird monitoring data collected over 6 years in managed forest and grassland areas across different regions in Germany, Central Europe. We combined these data with morphometric, ecological, behavioral, and acoustic trait data and detailed information on local land use management practices to understand how management decisions affect species and functional diversity, as well as ecological processes shaping local species composition. Our results reveal that the ecosystem and regional context must be considered to understand how management practices affect bird diversity aspects and composition. In forests, regional management decisions related to tree species and stand age affected bird diversity, as well as community and functional composition, and indicated environmental sorting due to ecological and behavioral requirements, biotic interactions, and morphometric constraints. In grasslands, independent of local management practices, increased intensity of land use resulted in an overall loss in bird species richness and functional diversity. Predominantly, constraints due to ecological or behavioral requirements affected bird species assemblage composition. In addition, our results indicated the importance of woody vegetation near managed grasslands and of considering environmental conditions beyond the local scale to support bird diversity and associated ecosystem functions. Our results highlighted that local management decisions can support bird diversity and maintain ecological function. However, this needs a view beyond the local scale of management units. It also demands a joint effort of biologists and land managers to integrate targeted conservation actions into regional management practices and create a network of habitats within production landscapes to protect nature, guard against biotic and functional homogenization, and prevent further degradation of ecosystems in production landscapes.

title: Dynamic Generalised Additive Models (DGAMs) for Forecasting Discrete Ecological Time Series
text:
Research article

Dynamic Generalised Additive Models (DGAMs) for Forecasting Discrete Ecological Time Series


Nicholas J. Clark, Konstans Wells


Full citation

Clark, N.J., & Wells, K. (2023). Dynamic Generalised Additive Models (DGAMs) for forecasting discrete ecological time series. Methods in Ecology and Evolution, 14, 771–784.



Publisher article



About this knowledge page. This BAHE Research Record provides curated notes and links for the peer-reviewed publication. It supports discovery and interpretation but does not replace the publisher-hosted version of record, which remains the authoritative scientific source and should be cited as the primary reference.








Research overview


The study advances ecological forecasting by demonstrating that nonlinear ecological processes and temporal dynamics can be modelled simultaneously within a unified probabilistic framework. By integrating hierarchical smooth functions with latent dynamic processes, DGAMs provide more accurate forecasts, improved uncertainty quantification and greater flexibility for analysing complex ecological time series than conventional generalised additive models.





Knowledge statements


Interpretive statements that distil key ideas and propositions from the publication to support scholarly reflection and discussion. They should be read alongside the complete peer-reviewed article rather than as standalone conclusions.


Knowledge statements are interpretive discussion points derived from the publication. They are intended to support exploration, comparison, and scholarly dialogue across the BAHE knowledge base. Individual statements are intentionally concise and should not be interpreted independently of the complete peer-reviewed publication, which remains the authoritative source for the authors arguments, evidence, and conclusions.






Dynamic Generalised Additive Models (DGAMs) integrate nonlinear ecological relationships with latent temporal processes within a unified Bayesian framework, enabling simultaneous inference and forecasting for discrete ecological time series.





Explicitly modelling latent temporal dynamics produces more robust and realistic ecological forecasts than extrapolating smooth functions beyond the range of observed data using conventional Generalised Additive Models.





Hierarchical dynamic factor models provide an efficient approach for forecasting multiple ecological time series by representing shared temporal dynamics through a reduced set of latent processes.





Bayesian probabilistic inference enables ecological forecasts to quantify uncertainty arising from nonlinear predictor effects, latent ecological dynamics and observation processes within a single coherent modelling framework.





Ecological forecasting models should accommodate incomplete observations, irregular sampling and discrete response distributions because these are fundamental characteristics of ecological time-series data rather than exceptional cases.





Reliable ecological forecasting requires integrating mechanistic ecological knowledge with dynamic statistical models that represent both biological processes and their associated uncertainties through time.






Original abstract


Abstract from the peer-reviewed publication.


Generalised additive models (GAMs) are powerful tools for modelling nonlinear ecological relationships but often perform poorly for forecasting because smooth functions extrapolate unrealistically beyond observed data. This paper introduces Dynamic Generalised Additive Models (DGAMs), which augment GAMs with latent dynamic processes to jointly model nonlinear predictor effects and temporal dependence. The framework is implemented in the open-source R package mvgam and supports Bayesian estimation of hierarchical smooth functions, dynamic latent factors and probabilistic forecasts for discrete ecological time series. Simulation studies and empirical applications demonstrate improved forecasting accuracy and uncertainty quantification relative to conventional GAMs.

title: Optimizing noninvasive sampling of a zoonotic bat virus
text:
Research article

Optimizing noninvasive sampling of a zoonotic bat virus


John R. Giles, Alison J. Peel, Konstans Wells, Raina K. Plowright, Hamish McCallum, Olivier Restif


Full citation

John R. Giles, Alison J. Peel, Konstans Wells, Raina K. Plowright, Hamish McCallum, Olivier Restif (2021). Optimizing noninvasive sampling of a zoonotic bat virus. Ecology and Evolution 11 12307–12321 https://doi.org/10.1002/ece3.7830



Publisher article



About this knowledge page. This BAHE Research Record provides curated notes and links for the peer-reviewed publication. It supports discovery and interpretation but does not replace the publisher-hosted version of record, which remains the authoritative scientific source and should be cited as the primary reference.








Research overview


This publication provides one of the first quantitative evaluations of bias arising from noninvasive pooled wildlife sampling and offers a general framework for optimizing surveillance designs for bat-borne zoonotic viruses.





Knowledge statements


Interpretive statements that distil key ideas and propositions from the publication to support scholarly reflection and discussion. They should be read alongside the complete peer-reviewed article rather than as standalone conclusions.


Knowledge statements are interpretive discussion points derived from the publication. They are intended to support exploration, comparison, and scholarly dialogue across the BAHE knowledge base. Individual statements are intentionally concise and should not be interpreted independently of the complete peer-reviewed publication, which remains the authoritative source for the authors arguments, evidence, and conclusions.






Conventional under-roost pooled sampling substantially overestimates bat-virus prevalence because samples receive excreta from multiple individuals.





The commonly used quadrant-sheet design estimated viral prevalence approximately 3.2 times higher than individual-level estimates.





Spatial autocorrelation among sampling sheets and clustering of bats are primary drivers of positive estimation bias.





Smaller, more numerous, spatially dispersed sampling sheets substantially reduce prevalence-estimation bias.





A stratified random sampling design using approximately 80–100 sheets of 0.75–1 m² provides a practical balance between estimation bias and false negatives.





Under-roost sampling is highly sensitive for pathogen detection but less suitable for unbiased prevalence estimation unless sampling design is optimized.





Spatial sample pooling creates an ecological aggregation bias that affects many wildlife disease surveillance systems.





Optimized noninvasive surveillance can improve monitoring of zoonotic spillover risk while minimizing disturbance to wildlife.

title: Join BAHE
text:
BAHE welcomes motivated researchers at different career stages who are driven by scientific curiosity, committed to rigorous ecological research and interested in understanding biodiversity, wildlife health, ecological interactions and socioecological systems in a rapidly changing world.
We particularly encourage individuals who enjoy integrating field observation, molecular and biodiversity data, conceptual thinking and quantitative analysis to address important ecological questions while contributing to biodiversity conservation, ecosystem resilience and sustainable futures.



Joining BAHE means becoming part of a collaborative, research-led environment where ecological questions drive methodological development and where field observation, biological understanding and quantitative analysis are integrated throughout the research process.
We aim to foster intellectual curiosity, scientific integrity and analytical rigour while encouraging researchers to think across disciplines and scales—from organisms and ecological interactions to ecosystems, environmental change and human wellbeing.
Researchers are encouraged to develop independence, collaborate openly and contribute to an evolving programme of research that advances both ecological understanding and evidence-based decision making.



Rather than recruiting only through advertised positions, BAHE welcomes discussions with outstanding early-career researchers interested in developing competitive fellowship applications.
We are particularly interested in researchers whose ideas align with BAHE’s interests in biodiversity, wildlife ecology, ecological interactions, One Health, quantitative ecology and sustainable futures.
Potential fellowship schemes include:

Royal Society University Research Fellowships
Royal Society Dorothy Hodgkin Fellowships
Marie Skłodowska-Curie Postdoctoral Fellowships
UK Research and Innovation (UKRI) fellowships
Leverhulme Early Career Fellowships
NERC Independent Research Fellowships
Wellcome Trust fellowships
Other appropriate national and international fellowship schemes

Prospective applicants are encouraged to contact us well in advance of application deadlines to discuss scientific fit, proposal development and institutional support.



BAHE welcomes prospective doctoral researchers who are excited by fundamental ecological questions and motivated to contribute to rigorous, interdisciplinary science.
Projects are normally developed collaboratively around shared scientific interests, available expertise and suitable funding opportunities rather than from predefined project lists.
Research interests currently include: - biodiversity responses to environmental change - wildlife ecology, parasites and microbiomes - ecological interactions and community assembly - One Health and disease ecology - ecological modelling and Bayesian statistics - biodiversity monitoring and evidence synthesis - restoration ecology and ecosystem resilience - nature, wellbeing and sustainable futures
Strong applicants typically demonstrate: - genuine scientific curiosity - excellent academic preparation - independent thinking and initiative - strong communication skills - quantitative, field or laboratory experience appropriate to their background - enthusiasm for interdisciplinary ecological research



The MRes provides intensive research experience within an active research environment and is an excellent pathway towards doctoral research, conservation science, quantitative ecology or related research careers.
Potential research areas include:

wildlife ecology and disease ecology
biodiversity monitoring and ecological modelling
urban ecology and One Health
tropical ecology and conservation
ecological interactions and ecological networks
microbiomes and host-associated communities
evidence synthesis and meta-analysis

Applicants commonly come from backgrounds including ecology, zoology, biology, environmental science, geography, conservation, veterinary biosciences, mathematics, statistics or data science.
For information about entry requirements, programme structure and university regulations, please see the Swansea University Biosciences MRes programme.



Subject to available capacity, BAHE welcomes enquiries from:

visiting PhD students
visiting postdoctoral researchers
undergraduate and postgraduate research placements
research interns
international visiting researchers
academic visitors interested in collaborative research

Visits are normally developed around shared research interests and ongoing projects.




Research philosophy: Curiosity about the diversity, complexity and resilience of the natural world is the starting point of scientific discovery and the motivation for understanding ecological change. BAHE integrates field observation, conceptual thinking, quantitative analysis and interdisciplinary synthesis to reveal how interactions among organisms, ecosystems and people shape biodiversity across scales. We aim to develop independent scientists who combine intellectual curiosity with analytical rigour, advancing ecological understanding while generating evidence that supports biodiversity conservation, ecosystem resilience and informed decisions for sustainable futures.







Ecological observation, biodiversity monitoring, wildlife sampling, study design and long-term ecological research.



R programming, Bayesian statistics, ecological modelling, simulation, reproducible workflows and ecological inference.



Conceptual development, hypothesis formulation, critical interpretation, evidence synthesis and interdisciplinary integration.



Scientific writing, visualisation, presentations, collaborative research, open science and knowledge exchange.



Connecting ecological understanding with biodiversity conservation, restoration, wildlife health and sustainable futures.



Developing original ideas, collaborative networks, grant writing and long-term scientific leadership.





Beyond academic achievement, we value researchers who demonstrate:

curiosity about the natural world
enthusiasm for ecological discovery
commitment to scientific excellence
integrity and openness in research
willingness to learn across disciplines
collaborative spirit
critical thinking and intellectual independence
motivation to contribute knowledge that benefits biodiversity and society




If you are interested in joining BAHE, please send a concise email including:

the type of opportunity you are interested in (Postdoctoral Fellowship, PhD, MRes, visiting researcher or placement);
a brief description of your research interests;
how your interests align with BAHE research;
relevant experience and technical skills;
information on existing or potential funding (where applicable);
a short curriculum vitae.


Strong enquiries usually demonstrate scientific curiosity, familiarity with relevant literature and a clear explanation of why BAHE provides an appropriate environment for developing the proposed research. We do not expect a complete research proposal, but we encourage applicants to identify one or two ecological questions they find particularly exciting and explain how they would like to explore them.




BAHE seeks to build a research community in which curiosity, scientific integrity and mutual respect provide the foundation for collaboration and discovery. We believe that excellent science is strengthened by diversity in people, disciplines, experiences, cultures and ideas, and that tackling complex ecological challenges requires openness to different perspectives and ways of thinking. We welcome talented researchers from all backgrounds who share a commitment to rigorous, collaborative and inclusive research.
Contact us

title: Join BAHE
text:
BAHE welcomes prospective doctoral researchers who are excited by fundamental ecological questions and motivated to contribute to rigorous, interdisciplinary science.
Projects are normally developed collaboratively around shared scientific interests, available expertise and suitable funding opportunities rather than from predefined project lists.
Research interests currently include: - biodiversity responses to environmental change - wildlife ecology, parasites and microbiomes - ecological interactions and community assembly - One Health and disease ecology - ecological modelling and Bayesian statistics - biodiversity monitoring and evidence synthesis - restoration ecology and ecosystem resilience - nature, wellbeing and sustainable futures
Strong applicants typically demonstrate: - genuine scientific curiosity - excellent academic preparation - independent thinking and initiative - strong communication skills - quantitative, field or laboratory experience appropriate to their background - enthusiasm for interdisciplinary ecological research

title: Join BAHE
text:
The MRes provides intensive research experience within an active research environment and is an excellent pathway towards doctoral research, conservation science, quantitative ecology or related research careers.
Potential research areas include:

wildlife ecology and disease ecology
biodiversity monitoring and ecological modelling
urban ecology and One Health
tropical ecology and conservation
ecological interactions and ecological networks
microbiomes and host-associated communities
evidence synthesis and meta-analysis

Applicants commonly come from backgrounds including ecology, zoology, biology, environmental science, geography, conservation, veterinary biosciences, mathematics, statistics or data science.
For information about entry requirements, programme structure and university regulations, please see the Swansea University Biosciences MRes programme.

title: Join BAHE
text: Research philosophy: Curiosity about the diversity, complexity and resilience of the natural world is the starting point of scientific discovery and the motivation for understanding ecological change. BAHE integrates field observation, conceptual thinking, quantitative analysis and interdisciplinary synthesis to reveal how interactions among organisms, ecosystems and people shape biodiversity across scales. We aim to develop independent scientists who combine intellectual curiosity with analytical rigour, advancing ecological understanding while generating evidence that supports biodiversity conservation, ecosystem resilience and informed decisions for sustainable futures.

title: BAHE Research Group
text:
Research programme

BAHE investigates how interactions among organisms, environments and people shape biodiversity, ecosystem functioning, wildlife health and sustainable futures. BAHE integrates ecological observation, molecular approaches, quantitative modelling and knowledge synthesis to understand how ecological systems function across scales.



Biodiversity under Global Change
We investigate how biodiversity responds to land-use change, habitat fragmentation, urbanisation, protected areas, renewable-energy landscapes, restoration and conservation intervention.


Wildlife ecology, health and One Health
We study wildlife populations, host–parasite interactions, microbiomes, zoonotic risk and disease ecology in ecological and conservation contexts, with long-standing interests in Borneo and tropical systems.


Ecological Interactions and Dynamics
We analyse how ecological interactions and dynamic processes unfold through time, including colonisation, succession, ecological networks, biodiversity offsetting, restoration trajectories and resilience.


Nature, Wellbeing and Sustainable Futures
We explore how nature exposure, nature connectedness and ecologically mediated wellbeing can be understood within broader One Health and social–ecological sustainability frameworks.

title: BAHE Research Group
text:
How we work
BAHE uses wildlife ecology as a foundation for understanding how interactions propagate across ecological and socioecological systems. By combining field ecology, molecular ecology, quantitative modelling and interdisciplinary collaboration, we seek to explain, predict and ultimately inform responses to biodiversity loss, wildlife health, restoration and sustainable futures. We view ecological systems as interconnected networks in which understanding interactions across biological, environmental and social scales creates new opportunities for scientific discovery and evidence-based solutions.

Our approach: field ecology + biodiversity data + ecological modelling + interaction networks + interdisciplinary collaboration.

title: BAHE Research Group
text:
Meet BAHE






Dr Konstans Wells
Principal Investigator
BAHE is coordinated by Dr Konstans Wells at Swansea University. The group works at the interface of wildlife ecology, biodiversity science, One Health and ecological system dynamics, with long-standing interests in mammals, birds, parasites, microbiomes and tropical systems.
The group is intentionally collaborative, combining field ecology, molecular ecology, bioinformatics, quantitative modelling and systems thinking with an international network of researchers, practitioners and policy partners.

title: Distinct Spread of DNA and RNA Viruses among Mammals amid Prominent Role of Domestic Species
text:
Research article

Distinct Spread of DNA and RNA Viruses among Mammals amid Prominent Role of Domestic Species


Konstans Wells, Serge Morand, Maya Wardeh, Matthew Baylis


Full citation

Wells, K., Morand, S., Wardeh, M., & Baylis, M. (2020). Distinct spread of DNA and RNA viruses among mammals amid prominent role of domestic species. Global Ecology and Biogeography, 29, 470–481.



Publisher article



About this knowledge page. This BAHE Research Record provides curated notes and links for the peer-reviewed publication. It supports discovery and interpretation but does not replace the publisher-hosted version of record, which remains the authoritative scientific source and should be cited as the primary reference.








Research overview


The study advances understanding of pathogen spillover by demonstrating that virus sharing emerges from interactions between host ecology, domestication and viral evolutionary characteristics rather than from a single host species alone. It shows that domestic mammals disproportionately connect wildlife and humans within global virus-sharing networks, RNA viruses have greater capacity for host sharing than DNA viruses, and probabilistic network modelling provides a robust framework for inferring pathogen-sharing processes from incomplete host–pathogen association data.





Knowledge statements


Interpretive statements that distil key ideas and propositions from the publication to support scholarly reflection and discussion. They should be read alongside the complete peer-reviewed article rather than as standalone conclusions.


Knowledge statements are interpretive discussion points derived from the publication. They are intended to support exploration, comparison, and scholarly dialogue across the BAHE knowledge base. Individual statements are intentionally concise and should not be interpreted independently of the complete peer-reviewed publication, which remains the authoritative source for the authors arguments, evidence, and conclusions.






Domestic mammals occupy central positions in global mammal–virus sharing networks, facilitating pathogen transmission between wildlife and humans.





RNA viruses exhibit broader host ranges and lower host specificity than DNA viruses, increasing their potential for cross-species transmission.





DNA viruses are generally more phylogenetically host-specific than RNA viruses, reflecting contrasting evolutionary dynamics of host switching.





Mammalian orders differ markedly in their contribution to virus-sharing networks, demonstrating that host taxonomy influences pathogen spread.





Network analyses integrating host and virus characteristics provide a powerful framework for understanding pathogen spillover across mammal communities.





Predicting future zoonotic emergence requires jointly considering host traits, domestication status and viral characteristics.





Reliable inference from global host–pathogen association networks requires probabilistic modelling that explicitly accounts for incomplete sampling and observation bias rather than treating observed interaction networks as complete representations of biological reality.






Original abstract


Abstract from the peer-reviewed publication.


Aim: Emerging infectious diseases arising from pathogen spillover from mammals to humans constitute a substantial health threat. Tracing virus origin and predicting the most likely host species for future spillover events are major objectives in One Health disciplines. We assessed patterns of virus sharing among a large diversity of mammals, including humans and domestic species. Location: Global. Time period: Current. Major taxa studied: Mammals and associated viruses. Methods: We used network centrality analysis and trait-based Bayesian hierarchical models to explore patterns of virus sharing among mammals. We analysed a global database that compiled the associations between 1,785 virus species and 725 mammalian host species as sourced from automatic screening of meta-data accompanying published nucleotide sequences between 1950 and 2019. Results: We show that based on current evidence, domesticated mammals hold the most central positions in networks of known mammal–virus associations. Among en- tire host–virus networks, Carnivora and Chiroptera hold central positions for mainly sharing RNA viruses, whereas ungulates hold central positions for sharing both RNA and DNA viruses with other host species. We revealed strong evidence that DNA viruses were phylogenetically more host specific than RNA viruses. RNA viruses ex- hibited low functional host specificity despite an overall tendency to infect phyloge- netically related species, signifying high potential to shift across hosts with different ecological niches. The frequencies of sharing viruses among hosts and the proportion of zoonotic viruses in hosts were larger for RNA than for DNA viruses. Main conclusions: Acknowledging the role of domestic species in addition to host and virus traits in patterns of virus sharing is necessary to improve our understand- ing of virus spread and spillover in times of global change. Understanding multi-host virus-sharing pathways adds focus to curtail disease spread

title: Managing Host–Parasite Interactions in Humans and Wildlife in Times of Global Change
text:
Review Article

Managing Host–Parasite Interactions in Humans and Wildlife in Times of Global Change


Konstans Wells, Robin Flynn


Full citation

Wells, K., & Flynn, R. (2022). Managing host–parasite interactions in humans and wildlife in times of global change. Parasitology Research, 121, 3063–3071. https://doi.org/10.1007/s00436-022-07649-7



Publisher article



About this knowledge page. This BAHE Research Record provides curated notes and links for the peer-reviewed publication. It supports discovery and interpretation but does not replace the publisher-hosted version of record, which remains the authoritative scientific source and should be cited as the primary reference.



Research overview


The publication develops a systems-level framework for parasite management under global change. It links within-host infection processes, among-host transmission, host population structure, environmental change, invasive species and control interventions within a unified eco-epidemiological perspective. It identifies two recurrent analytical pitfalls: targeting control solely according to observed prevalence and projecting parasite interactions across complete host ranges without considering environmental suitability and interaction plasticity.





Knowledge statements


Interpretive statements that distil key ideas and propositions from the publication to support scholarly reflection and discussion. They should be read alongside the complete peer-reviewed article rather than as standalone conclusions.


Knowledge statements are interpretive discussion points derived from the publication. They are intended to support exploration, comparison, and scholarly dialogue across the BAHE knowledge base. Individual statements are intentionally concise and should not be interpreted independently of the complete peer-reviewed publication, which remains the authoritative source for the authors arguments, evidence, and conclusions.






Global change can alter host–parasite interactions at every organisational level, from within-host physiology to population, community and ecosystem dynamics.





Parasite spread and control outcomes emerge from the interaction between within-host survival, among-host transmission and environmental heterogeneity.





Parasite control measures should be treated as components of dynamic host–parasite systems rather than as interventions acting independently of ecological context.





Climate change, habitat modification, urbanisation, pollution and species invasions can modify host abundance, connectivity, susceptibility, parasite persistence and transmission opportunities.





Human and domestic animal populations often experience increased connectivity and intensive parasite-control interventions, whereas many wildlife populations experience declining abundance, fragmentation and irregular access to control.





Differences in demographic structure, connectivity, exposure history and intervention intensity can cause host–parasite dynamics to differ fundamentally between humans, domestic animals and wildlife.





Control strategies that reduce infection in one host population can create unintended consequences in other populations through altered transmission, ecological interactions or antimicrobial resistance.





High parasite prevalence does not necessarily identify the location where intervention will most effectively contain spread, because prevalence can be high after the susceptible host pool has already declined.





Mapping host ranges alone can overestimate pathogen spillover risk when parasite persistence is restricted by environmental conditions, vector presence or local species assemblages.





Model-based forecasting should incorporate spatial and temporal variation, host connectivity, population structure, environmental suitability and uncertainty when evaluating parasite-control options.





Integrating surveillance, genomics, phylodynamics, immunological profiling, environmental monitoring and system-dynamical modelling can improve parasite-risk assessment and intervention design.





Major knowledge gaps remain in understanding how environmental stressors, host heterogeneity and multi-host interactions jointly shape parasite transmission, evolution and control outcomes.






Original abstract


Abstract from the peer-reviewed publication.


Global change in the Anthropocene has modified the environment of almost any species on earth, be it through climate change, habitat modifications, pollution, human intervention in the form of mass drug administration (MDA), or vaccination. This can have far-reaching consequences on all organisational levels of life, including eco-physiological stress at the cell and organism level, individual fitness and behaviour, population viability, species interactions and biodiversity. Host-parasite interactions often require highly adapted strategies by the parasite to survive and reproduce within the host environment and ensure efficient transmission among hosts. Yet, our understanding of the system-level outcomes of the intricate interplay of within host survival and among host parasite spread is in its infancy. We shed light on how global change affects host-parasite interactions at different organisational levels and address challenges and opportunities to work towards better-informed management of parasite control. We argue that global change affects host-parasite interactions in wildlife inhabiting natural environments rather differently than in humans and invasive species that benefit from anthropogenic environments as habitat and more deliberate rather than erratic exposure to therapeutic drugs and other control efforts.

title: The Day After Mowing: Time and Type of Mowing Influence Grassland Arthropods
text:
Research article

The Day After Mowing: Time and Type of Mowing Influence Grassland Arthropods


Johanna L. Berger, Michael Staab, Margarita Hartlieb, Nadja K. Simons, Konstans Wells, Martin M. Gossner, Juliane Vogt, Rafael Achury, Sebastian Seibold, Andreas Hemp, Wolfgang W. Weisser, Nico Blüthgen


Full citation

Berger, J. L., Staab, M., Hartlieb, M., Simons, N. K., Wells, K., Gossner, M. M., Vogt, J., Achury, R., Seibold, S., Hemp, A., Weisser, W. W., & Blüthgen, N. (2024). The day after mowing: Time and type of mowing influence grassland arthropods. Ecological Applications, 34(6), e3022. https://doi.org/10.1002/eap.3022



Publisher article



About this knowledge page. This BAHE Research Record provides curated notes and links for the peer-reviewed publication. It supports discovery and interpretation but does not replace the publisher-hosted version of record, which remains the authoritative scientific source and should be cited as the primary reference.




Grassland arthropod abundance recovers progressively after mowing, with responses differing among arthropod orders and mowing techniques.




Research overview


The study demonstrates that the timing of mowing is a dominant driver of grassland arthropod abundance and diversity under real-world agricultural management. By analysing detailed mowing practices alongside grazing, fertilization, rolling and leveling, it distinguishes the large disturbance effect of mowing itself from smaller effects of mower design. The results indicate that recovery after mowing is gradual and likely depends on recolonization, while some diversity losses persist beyond simple reductions in individual abundance.





Knowledge statements


Interpretive statements that distil key ideas and propositions from the publication to support scholarly reflection and discussion. They should be read alongside the complete peer-reviewed article rather than as standalone conclusions.


Knowledge statements are interpretive discussion points derived from the publication. They are intended to support exploration, comparison, and scholarly dialogue across the BAHE knowledge base. Individual statements are intentionally concise and should not be interpreted independently of the complete peer-reviewed publication, which remains the authoritative source for the authors arguments, evidence, and conclusions.






Time elapsed since mowing is the dominant driver of grassland arthropod abundance and diversity, outweighing the influence of most other measured grassland management practices.





Mowing causes an immediate, large-scale reduction in arthropod abundance, but populations recover progressively, reaching approximately three- to seven-fold higher abundance within 100 days after mowing.





Mowing reduces arthropod diversity through mechanisms that extend beyond simple declines in abundance, indicating that disturbance selectively removes species as well as individuals.





Among common mowing practices, mulching has the most consistently negative effects on grassland arthropods, whereas changes to mower design or settings generally have comparatively limited benefits.





Recovery following mowing is likely driven by recolonization from surrounding unmown habitats and depends on species-specific dispersal ability and landscape connectivity.





Conserving grassland arthropods is likely to depend more on reducing the frequency and spatial extent of mowing and maintaining unmown refuges than on incremental optimization of mowing equipment.






Original abstract


Abstract from the peer-reviewed publication.


Recent losses in the abundance and diversity of arthropods have been documented in many regions and ecosystems. In grasslands, such insect declines are largely attributed to land use, including modern machinery and mowing regimes. However, the effects of different mowing techniques on arthropods remain poorly understood. Using 11 years of data from 111 agricultural grassland plots across Germany, we analyzed the influence of various grassland management variables on the abundance and abundance-accounted species richness of four arthropod orders: Araneae, Coleoptera, Hemiptera, and Orthoptera. The analysis focused on detailed mowing information, for example, days after mowing and mower type, and compared their effect with other aspects of grassland management, that is, rolling, leveling, fertilization, and grazing. We found strong negative effects of mowing on all four arthropod orders, with arthropod abundance being lowest directly after mowing and steadily increasing to three to seven times the abundance after 100 days post-mowing. Likewise, Hemiptera and Coleoptera species richness was 30% higher 100 days after mowing. Mower width showed a positive effect on Orthoptera abundance, but not on the other arthropods. Arthropod abundance and Coleoptera species richness were lowest when a mulcher was used compared to rotary or bar mowers. In addition to mowing, intensive grazing negatively affected Orthoptera abundance but not the other orders. Mowing represents a highly disturbing and iterative stressor with negative effects on arthropod abundance and diversity, likely contributed by mowing-induced mortality and habitat alteration. While modifications of mowing techniques such as mower type or mowing height and width may help to reduce the negative impact of mowing on arthropods, our results show that mowing itself has the most substantial negative effect. Based on our results, we suggest that reduced mowing frequency, omission of mowing in parts of the grassland (refuges), or extensive grazing instead of mowing have the greatest potential to promote arthropod populations.

title: Modelling nonlinear responses of a desert rodent species to environmental change with hierarchical dynamic generalized additive models
